channel.title: Comments for page "Intelligence Agency Data Archive — Subject: Mages"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Intelligence Agency Data Archive — Subject: Mages"

item.title: No-vote.
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed most of the tale right until the end. The last part feels rushed to me and it kind of comes out of nowhere. There was no buildup that the rest knew he was a spy it just he gets reveal and he gets caught. It just doesn't feel right to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have a two core issues with this piece:

First, the technical layout of the piece is somewhat disorienting. Some actions getting their own lines separate from any dialogue going on never feels right to me (see: "Straightening his tie, North looked over his clipboard.") Reading it is awkward. On a related note, many of these actions are so mundane that their inclusion isn't particularly satisfying. In "Disappointed with the current results, Nine listed off his questions", you don't need to tell me that questions are being listed off if you write them immediately afterwards, and the disappointment could have been implied in their wording. Similarly, in "Morgan Parker gave North a puzzled look" the preceding question already implies puzzled feelings. That's not to say that everything needs to be described in lengthy detail (or that nothing should be described at all), but simple stage directions should be used sparingly and for things that aren't directly implied by the text.

Second, I think you're trying to fit too much into this one tale, or at least trying to tell too meaty a story with too few words. The characters feel relatively insubstantial and I wasn't invested in the twists and turns of their lives as a result. The entertainment value of the events themselves are similarly undercut by a lack of setup and detail. I'm all for a good action scene, or even colleagues shooting the shit at lunch, but I need a few interesting details in each for it to stick in my mind. Depict things that help the reader understand them as people (appearances, personal quirks, possessions, etc.) and set up the scenes for more kinetic bits. Create the conceptual skeleton that my imagination's going to graft flesh to, if I'm being flowery.

Generally speaking, my advice would be to cut out the less interesting details (stage directions that could be intuited mostly,) and flesh out the events of key plot points more. Give a better impression of characters' motivations and the history of their relationships, contextualize the events of the piece more, and maybe just mix up the sentence structure more too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creating a canon for it.

This lays out most the ground work for what I have in mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I never understood why people don't use the Foundation's Intelligence Division more often. Even little things like putting their logo at the top of one of those "recovered documents" that we have so many of.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
“Ten Points to Thaumiel” strikes again with another article! This is an entry for the GoI-Int contest based on the GoI Mages Academy from the German Branch. Found Here: http://scp-int.wikidot.com/magierakademie-hub

Huge thanks to AthosNetwork, Ellie3, pieiswrong, Stallmantic, and Wydness for all the feedback they left for me to help me improve. As well as anyone who pointed out grammar checks in either Discord or IRC chat.
